title: Proactive Outpatient PSYCHIATRIC ADVANCE DIRECTIVE (PAD) for a STABLE, Capable 41-Year-Old Man with Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type) Maintained on a Long-Acting Injectable Antipsychotic and Lithium: Documenting Treatment Preferences for a FUTURE Crisis (Preferred and Refused Medications WITH the Reasons, Hospital and ECT Preferences, De-escalation/Seclusion/Restraint Preferences, Visitors, and Care of Dependents), Naming and EDUCATING a Health-Care Agent for Mental-Health Decisions, Confirming PRESENT Capacity, Clarifying WHEN a PAD Activates and Its Legal LIMITS, and Ensuring Cross-Setting Portability — All Without Coercion
subtitle: A PSYCHIATRIC ADVANCE DIRECTIVE (PAD) is a legal document a person with a serious mental illness completes WHILE STABLE AND CAPABLE to record their treatment preferences and/or to name a health-care agent for use during a FUTURE mental-health crisis in which they may temporarily LOSE decision-making capacity. It is the mental-health parallel to a medical advance directive and, done PROACTIVELY in the outpatient setting during a stable phase (not in the middle of an acute episode), it preserves autonomy, reduces coercion, can shorten crises, and helps the crisis/inpatient team treat the patient the way the patient themselves chose when well. This case is the proactive outpatient PAD conversation with a CAPABLE patient — distinct from proactive MEDICAL advance care planning/POLST (case-142), involuntary civil commitment + duty to warn (case-139), and informed consent for ECT (case-147), though a PAD can be combined with a medical advance directive. TWO COMPONENTS apply, mirroring a medical AD. (A) An INSTRUCTIONAL directive states the patient's psychiatric treatment PREFERENCES: medications that have WORKED and those to REFUSE or AVOID and WHY (e.g., 'I had an acute dystonic reaction to IM haloperidol — avoid it; I respond well to my long-acting injectable and to oral olanzapine'), preferred hospital/facility, preferences about ELECTROCONVULSIVE THERAPY, de-escalation strategies that calm them and preferences about SECLUSION/RESTRAINT, who may and may NOT visit or be notified, and practical instructions for care of dependents/pets/bills/housing during hospitalization, plus their personal EARLY WARNING SIGNS of relapse. (B) A HEALTH-CARE POWER OF ATTORNEY for mental health NAMES an AGENT/PROXY to make psychiatric decisions when the patient cannot — high yield, because no document anticipates every scenario and a well-EDUCATED agent can apply SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT. CONFIRM PRESENT CAPACITY first: capacity is decision-specific and PRESUMED; a chronic diagnosis of schizoaffective disorder, prior hospitalizations, taking psychiatric medication, an unwise choice, or simple disagreement do NOT by themselves equal incapacity (link to [[case-116]] and [[case-142]]). The PAD must be authored during a period of capacity — NOT during acute psychosis or mania. ACTIVATION: a PAD becomes OPERATIVE only when the patient is determined to LACK capacity (by a clinician/process per state law), and it is SUSPENDED again once capacity returns — the capable patient otherwise speaks for themselves. LEGAL LIMITS (teach honestly): a PAD generally CANNOT compel treatment that is not clinically indicated or available, cannot force the system to hospitalize the patient, and cannot override emergency treatment needed to prevent serious imminent harm or a valid court commitment order; a clinician MAY decline to follow a specific PAD instruction that is not clinically appropriate or feasible but must DOCUMENT the clinical reason. Many states allow ADVANCE CONSENT to treatment over a future incapacitated objection (a key value of PADs — it lets the well self authorize the unwell self's care), and REVOCATION rules are STATE-SPECIFIC: many states allow revocation ONLY while the patient has capacity (so a crisis-time refusal does not automatically void the PAD), while others allow revocation at any time — teach the patient their state's rule. Documents are STATE-SPECIFIC (witnesses/notary/forms). PORTABILITY is the most common operational failure: a perfect PAD that the crisis team cannot find does nothing — file it in the chart/EHR and any state PAD/crisis registry, give copies to the agent and outpatient psychiatrist, share it with the preferred hospital ED and the local crisis team, and provide the patient a wallet card. Frame the PAD as an ONGOING PROCESS to revisit when treatment response, supports, or wishes change. Respect AUTONOMY including the right to DEFER or DECLINE; never coerce and never have the patient sign what they do not understand. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: treating the schizoaffective diagnosis as automatic incapacity and bypassing him; trying to author the PAD during an acute episode; pressuring specific choices or having him sign what he doesn't understand; promising the PAD can FORCE the team to hospitalize him or can ALWAYS override a safety-driven emergency/valid commitment; completing the document but never making it portable; and, later, ignoring a valid, applicable PAD during a crisis. NURSING ROLE: facilitate the conversation while the patient is stable, confirm present capacity, elicit treatment history and preferences in plain language, support agent selection and EDUCATION, teach activation/limits/revocation accurately, coordinate execution per state law, ensure cross-setting portability, honor a valid applicable PAD during a future crisis, and document objectively.

patient.name: D. Okafor
patient.code: Outpatient (community mental-health clinic, stable maintenance visit); full code currently; goal = proactive creation of a PSYCHIATRIC ADVANCE DIRECTIVE with a CAPABLE patient in the stable phase — document treatment preferences for a future crisis, name and educate a mental-health agent, confirm present capacity, teach activation/limits/revocation, execute per state law, and ensure cross-setting portability without coercion
patient.allergies: NKDA (no true drug allergy); ADVERSE REACTION on record: acute dystonic reaction (oculogyric crisis + torticollis) to IM HALOPERIDOL during a prior ED crisis visit
patient.admitDate: Seen at his community mental-health clinic for a routine maintenance visit. Dx: schizoaffective disorder, BIPOLAR TYPE. STABLE x ~14 months on paliperidone palmitate monthly long-acting injectable (LAI) + lithium; euthymic, no current psychosis, working part-time, lives independently. PMH of 3 prior psychiatric hospitalizations during manic-psychotic episodes; during one ED visit he received IM haloperidol and had an acute dystonic reaction. He is alert, organized, and says: 'When I get sick I can't think straight, and the last hospital used a medication that hurt me. I want to write down what I want BEFORE the next time, while I'm doing well.' The clinic RN facilitates creation of a PSYCHIATRIC ADVANCE DIRECTIVE (capacity confirmation, treatment-history and preference elicitation, agent selection and education, teaching on activation/limits/revocation, document execution per state law, and portability) without coercion, respecting his autonomy.
patient.room: Outpatient community mental-health clinic; private office; interdisciplinary support available (psychiatrist, clinic RN, social worker/peer-support specialist, legal aid for document execution); patient present and capable; his sister (proposed agent) joins by phone

vitals.temp: 36.7°C; alert, calm, cooperative, euthymic affect; thought process linear and goal-directed; NO current psychosis, mania, or suicidal/homicidal ideation; stable x ~14 months on LAI + lithium; explicit interest in creating a psychiatric advance directive while well

36.6°C; demonstrates capacity (understands the PAD, appreciates how it applies to a future episode, reasons through options, communicates stable choices); chronic schizoaffective diagnosis alone does NOT equal incapacity; relates clear treatment history — LAI + olanzapine helpful, IM haloperidol caused acute dystonia, dislikes seclusion but is calmed by a quiet low-stimulation room and being told the plan

vitals.time: Education — what a PAD is, components, activation, limits, revocation
vitals.temp: 36.7°C; nurse teaches in plain language: a PAD = instructional preferences + a named mental-health AGENT, authored while CAPABLE, that ACTIVATES only when he is later found to lack capacity and is SUSPENDED when capacity returns; honest teaching on LIMITS (cannot force hospitalization, cannot override a safety emergency/valid commitment, clinician may decline a non-indicated instruction with documented reason) and state-specific REVOCATION rules

36.6°C; names his sister as mental-health AGENT and EDUCATES her about his values and preferences so she can apply substituted judgment; crisis plan documented — preferred LAI/oral olanzapine, AVOID IM haloperidol, preferred hospital, ECT preference recorded, de-escalation (quiet room, explain the plan, avoid seclusion if possible), visitors/notification list, and instructions for care of his dog and rent during any hospitalization

36.7°C; PAD executed per state-specific law (witnesses/notary as required); copies filed in chart/EHR and the state PAD/crisis registry, given to the patient and to his sister/agent, and shared with his outpatient psychiatrist, his preferred hospital ED, and the local mobile crisis team; he carries a wallet card noting he has a PAD and where to find it

vitals.time: Follow-up — ongoing process (and honored in a later crisis)
vitals.temp: 36.7°C; PAD framed as an ongoing PROCESS to revisit when treatment response/supports/wishes change; he understands he can update it anytime while capable; documentation complete and the team is aligned — and when he was later hospitalized in a manic-psychotic relapse, the inpatient team retrieved the PAD, used his LAI/oral olanzapine, AVOIDED haloperidol, and engaged his sister/agent

labs.n: PRESENTATION + the proactive outpatient PSYCHIATRIC ADVANCE DIRECTIVE (PAD) framework
labs.v: 41 y/o man D. Okafor with schizoaffective disorder (bipolar type), STABLE x ~14 months on a paliperidone palmitate monthly LAI + lithium, euthymic and capable, at a routine community-mental-health visit, who wants to create a PSYCHIATRIC ADVANCE DIRECTIVE 'while I'm doing well.' A PAD lets a competent person with serious mental illness record psychiatric treatment preferences and/or name a mental-health agent for use during a FUTURE crisis in which capacity may be lost. Done PROACTIVELY in the stable phase (not during an acute episode), it preserves autonomy, reduces coercion, can shorten crises, and lets the crisis/inpatient team honor the choices the patient made when well. It is distinct from medical advance care planning/POLST ([[case-142]]), involuntary commitment + duty to warn ([[case-139]]), and ECT consent ([[case-147]]) — though a PAD may be combined with a medical AD. The nurse's flow: confirm present capacity -> elicit treatment history and preferences in plain language -> teach the components, activation, limits, and revocation -> support agent selection + EDUCATION -> coordinate execution per state law -> ensure cross-setting portability -> frame as an ongoing process. Throughout: respect autonomy (including the right to defer/decline) and document objectively.
labs.r: PAD = a competent person with serious mental illness records psychiatric treatment preferences + names a mental-health agent for a FUTURE incapacitated crisis. Authored WHILE STABLE/capable. Flow: confirm capacity -> elicit history/preferences -> teach components/activation/limits/revocation -> agent selection + EDUCATION -> execute per state law -> portability -> ongoing process. Respect autonomy (incl. right to defer/decline); document. RN role: facilitate without coercion.

labs.n: What a PAD IS — the two components + ACTIVATION (when it takes effect)
labs.v: TWO components, mirroring a medical AD. (A) INSTRUCTIONAL directive — the patient's psychiatric treatment PREFERENCES: medications that have WORKED and those to REFUSE/AVOID and WHY (here: prefers his LAI and oral olanzapine; AVOID IM haloperidol after an acute dystonic reaction), preferred HOSPITAL/facility, preference about ELECTROCONVULSIVE THERAPY, de-escalation strategies that calm him and preferences about SECLUSION/RESTRAINT, WHO may visit or be notified (and who may not), and practical instructions for care of dependents/pets/bills/housing during hospitalization, plus his personal EARLY WARNING SIGNS of relapse. (B) HEALTH-CARE POWER OF ATTORNEY for mental health — NAMES an AGENT/PROXY to make psychiatric decisions when he cannot; high-yield because no document anticipates every scenario, and a well-EDUCATED agent can apply SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what HE would want). ACTIVATION: a PAD is authored while the person HAS capacity but becomes OPERATIVE only when the patient is later determined to LACK capacity (by a clinician/process per state law); it is SUSPENDED once capacity returns — at all other times the capable patient speaks for themselves. The PAD must NOT be authored during acute psychosis or mania.
labs.r: TWO components: (A) INSTRUCTIONAL preferences = preferred/refused meds WITH reasons, hospital choice, ECT preference, de-escalation + seclusion/restraint preferences, visitors/notification, dependent/pet/bill care, early warning signs; (B) AGENT (mental-health POA) who applies SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT. ACTIVATION: authored while CAPABLE; operative ONLY when capacity is later lost; SUSPENDED when capacity returns. Never author during acute psychosis/mania. RN role: elicit preferences + support agent designation.

labs.n: Capacity to execute + LEGAL LIMITS of a PAD + state-specific REVOCATION
labs.v: CAPACITY (decision-specific, presumed): the four-element test — understand the information, appreciate how it applies to him, reason through options, and communicate a stable choice; a chronic schizoaffective diagnosis, prior hospitalizations, taking psychiatric medication, an unwise choice, or disagreement do NOT by themselves equal incapacity ([[case-116]], [[case-142]]). The PAD must be created during a period of capacity. LEGAL LIMITS (teach honestly, do not over-promise): a PAD generally CANNOT compel treatment that is not clinically indicated or available; CANNOT force the system to hospitalize him; and CANNOT override emergency treatment necessary to prevent serious imminent harm, or a valid court civil-commitment order. A clinician MAY decline to follow a specific PAD instruction that is not clinically appropriate/feasible, but must DOCUMENT the clinical reason. ADVANCE CONSENT: many states let a PAD give consent to treatment over a future INCAPACITATED objection — a central value of a PAD, letting the well self authorize the unwell self's care. REVOCATION is STATE-SPECIFIC: many states allow revocation ONLY while the patient has capacity (so a crisis-time refusal does not automatically void the PAD); others allow revocation at any time — teach him HIS state's rule. Documents are state-specific (witnesses/notary/forms): execute per state law and review when moving states.
labs.r: CAPACITY = decision-specific, presumed: understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a stable choice (schizoaffective dx / prior hospitalizations / taking meds / unwise choice / disagreement != incapacity); author while capable. LIMITS: a PAD can't compel non-indicated/unavailable care, can't force hospitalization, can't override a safety emergency or valid commitment; clinician may decline an instruction WITH documented reason. Many states allow ADVANCE CONSENT over future objection. REVOCATION is state-specific (often only while capable). RN role: teach limits/revocation honestly.

labs.n: Execution + cross-setting PORTABILITY + ongoing process + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: EXECUTION: follow state-specific requirements (witnesses, notary, forms); the patient signs while capable. PORTABILITY (the most common operational failure point): a perfect PAD the crisis team cannot find does nothing — file it in the chart/EHR and any state PAD/crisis registry; give COPIES to the patient and to the agent (his sister); share with the outpatient psychiatrist, the PREFERRED HOSPITAL ED, and the local mobile crisis team; provide a wallet card noting he has a PAD and where it is. FOLLOW-UP: a PAD is an ongoing PROCESS — revisit when treatment response, supports, or wishes change, and remind him he can update it any time while capable. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT treat his schizoaffective diagnosis as automatic incapacity and bypass him; did NOT try to author the PAD during an acute episode; did NOT pressure specific choices or have him sign what he did not understand; did NOT promise the PAD could FORCE hospitalization or ALWAYS override a safety emergency/valid commitment; and did NOT complete the document without making it portable. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: proactive timing during a stable, capable phase; capacity confirmed; values/treatment-history conversation in plain language; accurate teaching of components, activation, limits, and revocation; an agent selected and EDUCATED about his values; documents executed per state law and made portable across settings; ongoing-process framing; objective documentation. EXPECTED outcome: his autonomy is preserved, his crisis preferences are clear and accessible, his agent is prepared to advocate, and — as occurred at his next relapse — the team honored the PAD (used his LAI/oral olanzapine, AVOIDED haloperidol, engaged his agent), shortening and de-escalating the crisis.
labs.r: EXECUTION per state law (witnesses/notary); patient signs while capable. PORTABILITY = the common failure: chart/EHR + state PAD/crisis registry + copies to patient/agent + share w/ outpatient psychiatrist + preferred hospital ED + mobile crisis team + wallet card. FOLLOW-UP = ongoing process; update anytime while capable. Negatives confirm correct practice (didn't equate dx w/ incapacity, didn't author during an episode, didn't coerce/over-promise force-hospitalization, didn't skip portability). Protective: proactive stable-phase timing + capacity + plain-language preferences + educated agent + portable documents + ongoing process. RN role: execute + portability + honor in crisis.

labs.v: 0.8 mEq/L (therapeutic maintenance range; consistent with a stable euthymic phase)
2.1 mIU/L (normal; routine lithium monitoring — lithium can cause hypothyroidism)
0.9 mg/dL; eGFR > 90 mL/min/1.73 m^2 (normal renal function; routine lithium monitoring)
Negative (no substance intoxication contributing to today's presentation; supports an intact-capacity, stable-phase encounter appropriate for authoring a PAD)

nursesNote: Outpatient community-mental-health clinic — routine maintenance visit. 41 y/o man, schizoaffective disorder (bipolar type), STABLE x ~14 months on paliperidone palmitate monthly LAI + lithium (level 0.8 mEq/L; TSH/renal normal; tox negative). Alert, calm, euthymic, linear thought, NO psychosis/mania/SI/HI. He requests creation of a PSYCHIATRIC ADVANCE DIRECTIVE 'while I'm doing well.' Sister (proposed agent) on speakerphone. PAD flow: (1) CAPACITY confirmed (understands/appreciates/reasons/communicates a stable choice; chronic schizoaffective dx + prior hospitalizations + taking meds do NOT equal incapacity); must be authored while capable, not during an episode. (2) TREATMENT HISTORY + PREFERENCES elicited in plain language — prefers his LAI and oral olanzapine; AVOID IM haloperidol (prior acute dystonic reaction/oculogyric crisis); preferred hospital recorded; ECT preference recorded; de-escalation = quiet low-stimulation room + explain the plan, prefers to avoid seclusion; visitors/notification list; instructions for care of his dog + rent during any hospitalization; personal early warning signs. (3) EDUCATED on the two components (instructional preferences + named mental-health AGENT) and on ACTIVATION (operative only when capacity is later lost; suspended when capacity returns), with HONEST teaching of LIMITS (a PAD cannot force hospitalization, cannot override a safety emergency or valid commitment, and a clinician may decline a non-indicated instruction with documented reason) and state-specific REVOCATION (often only while capable). (4) AGENT — sister named and EDUCATED about his values so she can apply SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT. No coercion; right to defer/decline respected. (5) EXECUTION per state law (witnesses/notary as required); patient signed while capable. (6) PORTABILITY — copies in chart/EHR + state PAD/crisis registry, given to patient + sister/agent, shared with outpatient psychiatrist + preferred hospital ED + mobile crisis team; wallet card carried. (7) Framed as an ongoing PROCESS to revisit; he can update anytime while capable. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT treat the diagnosis as automatic incapacity, did NOT author during an acute episode, did NOT pressure choices or have him sign what he didn't understand, did NOT promise the PAD could force hospitalization or override a safety emergency, did NOT skip portability. Documentation objective and contemporaneous; team aligned; agent prepared.
providerOrders: VISIT: routine outpatient maintenance visit, community mental-health clinic; element = proactive creation of a psychiatric advance directive (PAD) with a stable, capable patient

CAPACITY: confirm present decision-making capacity (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a stable choice); document that a chronic schizoaffective diagnosis, prior hospitalizations, or taking psychiatric medication do NOT by themselves equal incapacity; author the PAD only while capacity is intact

FACILITATION: support a values-based, plain-language PAD conversation; respect autonomy including the right to defer or decline; do not coerce or have the patient sign documents he does not understand

PREFERENCES: elicit and document treatment history and preferences — preferred and refused medications WITH reasons (prefers LAI + oral olanzapine; AVOID IM haloperidol after acute dystonic reaction), preferred hospital, ECT preference, de-escalation strategies + seclusion/restraint preferences, visitors/notification list, care of dependents/pet/bills/housing, and personal early warning signs

EDUCATION: teach the two PAD components (instructional preferences + named mental-health agent), ACTIVATION (operative only when capacity is later lost; suspended when capacity returns), and an HONEST account of LIMITS (cannot compel non-indicated/unavailable care, cannot force hospitalization, cannot override a safety emergency or valid civil-commitment order; clinician may decline a non-indicated instruction with documented reason) and state-specific REVOCATION rules

AGENT: support selection of a mental-health health-care agent (and alternate as desired) and EDUCATE the agent about the patient's values so they can apply substituted judgment; involve the agent/family with the patient's consent
DOCUMENT: complete and execute the PAD per state-specific law (witnesses/notary as required); coordinate legal-aid/social-work assistance as needed

PORTABILITY: file copies in the chart/EHR and any state PAD/crisis registry; provide copies to the patient and the agent; share with the outpatient psychiatrist, the preferred hospital ED, and the local mobile crisis team; provide a wallet card indicating a PAD exists and where to find it
FOLLOW-UP: frame the PAD as an ongoing process — revisit when treatment response, supports, or wishes change; remind the patient he can update it any time while capable

DOCUMENTATION: objective, contemporaneous record of capacity, treatment history and preferences elicited (in the patient's own words), education delivered, agent selection and education, document execution, and where copies were sent

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters about this proactive PAD visit?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: A capable patient with serious mental illness, currently STABLE, who wants to create a psychiatric advance directive — the ideal time to do it is now, while he can speak for himself
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a PAD must be authored during a capable, stable phase; proactive timing is what makes it valid and useful. RN role: facilitate proactively.

opts.text: He has a documented adverse reaction (acute dystonia to IM haloperidol) and a clear sense of which treatments help — exactly the kind of preference a PAD should capture
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — preferred/refused medications WITH the reasons are core PAD content and directly improve future crisis care. RN role: elicit treatment history.

opts.text: Capacity is presumed and decision-specific; a chronic schizoaffective diagnosis, prior hospitalizations, and taking psychiatric medication do NOT by themselves equal incapacity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirming present capacity keeps the patient at the center; the diagnosis alone never strips decision rights. RN role: confirm capacity.

opts.text: A PAD has two parts — instructional treatment preferences AND a named mental-health agent — and the agent must be educated about his values
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a named but uninformed agent cannot apply substituted judgment. RN role: support agent selection + education.

opts.text: A PAD activates only when he is later found to LACK capacity and is suspended when capacity returns — and it has legal limits he should understand honestly
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — accurate teaching of activation and limits prevents false expectations (e.g., that it can force hospitalization). RN role: teach activation/limits.

opts.text: The document must be executed per state law and made PORTABLE so the crisis/inpatient team can actually find it during a future episode
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a PAD the team cannot locate during a crisis does nothing; portability is the most common operational failure. RN role: ensure portability.

opts.text: Because he has schizoaffective disorder, defer to his sister to make all of his treatment decisions now and have her complete the document for him
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. He is capable; bypassing a capable patient ignores his autonomy and the diagnosis alone does not equal incapacity.

opts.text: Tell him the PAD will guarantee he can never be hospitalized against his wishes and will always override the crisis team
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A PAD cannot force or forbid hospitalization and cannot override a safety emergency or a valid civil-commitment order; over-promising misinforms him.

opts.text: A PAD is authored WHILE the person has capacity and becomes operative only when capacity is later lost; it is suspended again once capacity returns
opts.rat: CORRECT — the activation principle. It lets the well self direct care for the unwell self, then steps aside when he recovers.

opts.text: A PAD has an instructional component (treatment preferences) and may name a mental-health agent who applies substituted judgment if he cannot decide
opts.rat: CORRECT — the two components. A well-educated agent is often the most useful piece because no document anticipates every scenario.

opts.text: Capacity is decision-specific and presumed; schizoaffective disorder, prior hospitalizations, an unwise choice, or disagreement do not by themselves equal incapacity
opts.rat: CORRECT — capacity principles. Keeps a capable patient at the center of his own planning.

opts.text: A PAD cannot compel treatment that is not clinically indicated or available, cannot force hospitalization, and cannot override emergency treatment for imminent harm or a valid commitment order
opts.rat: CORRECT — the legal limits. A clinician may decline a non-indicated instruction but must document the clinical reason.

opts.text: Revocation rules are state-specific — many states allow revocation only while the patient has capacity, so a crisis-time refusal does not automatically void the PAD
opts.rat: CORRECT — revocation principle. This is precisely what protects the document's value during a future loss of capacity; teach his state's rule.

opts.text: A PAD is an ongoing process; it should be revisited when treatment response, supports, or wishes change, and he can update it anytime while capable
opts.rat: CORRECT — process/revisit principle. Sustains alignment between the document and his current values.

opts.text: A valid PAD can be created at any time, including during an acute psychotic or manic episode, because the diagnosis itself authorizes it
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A PAD must be authored while the person has capacity; an acute episode is when capacity is most likely impaired — the wrong time to execute it.

opts.text: Once a person is psychiatrically hospitalized or committed, they automatically lose the right to make any of their own decisions, so a PAD is pointless
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Capacity is a clinical determination separate from admission/commitment status; a PAD exists precisely to guide care if and when capacity is lost ([[case-139]]).

opts.text: Confirm present decision-making capacity and elicit his treatment history, values, and preferences in plain language
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Capacity and his own preferences are the foundation of a valid, useful PAD. RN role: confirm capacity + elicit preferences.

opts.text: Teach the components, activation, legal limits, and revocation rules accurately and honestly
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Informed choice requires an accurate understanding of what the PAD can and cannot do. RN role: teach activation/limits/revocation.

opts.text: Support selection of a mental-health agent and EDUCATE the agent about his values
opts.rat: #3 — A named but uninformed agent cannot apply substituted judgment. RN role: agent selection + education.

opts.text: Execute the document per state law and ensure cross-setting portability so it is accessible in a future crisis
opts.rat: #4 — Execution + portability turn the conversation into care that actually follows him. RN role: execute + make portable.

opts.text: Frame the PAD as an ongoing process, schedule reassessment, and document objectively
opts.rat: #5 — Sustains alignment between the document and his evolving values. RN role: revisit + document.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this PAD visit.

opts.text: Confirm present capacity and elicit his treatment history and preferences (preferred/refused meds with reasons, hospital, ECT, de-escalation, visitors, care of dependents) in plain language
opts.rat: Capacity and his own preferences anchor a valid PAD; plain language supports informed choice. RN role: capacity + preferences.

opts.text: Teach the two components, when the PAD activates and is suspended, its legal limits, and his state's revocation rules
opts.rat: Accurate education enables an informed, realistic document. RN role: teach activation/limits/revocation.

opts.text: Support selection of a mental-health agent and educate the agent about his values so they can apply substituted judgment
opts.rat: An informed agent is essential to substituted judgment in a future crisis. RN role: agent selection + education.

opts.text: Execute the PAD per state law and ensure portability — chart/EHR + state registry + copies to patient/agent + share with psychiatrist, preferred hospital ED, and crisis team + wallet card
opts.rat: A valid document that the crisis team can actually find is the operational output; portability is the most common failure point. RN role: execute + make portable.

opts.text: Respect his autonomy — including the right to defer or decline — and frame the PAD as an ongoing process to revisit
opts.rat: Autonomy includes the right to decline; a PAD is a process, not a one-time form. RN role: respect + revisit.

opts.text: Document the AVOID-haloperidol instruction prominently and his preference for his LAI / oral olanzapine and de-escalation strategies
opts.rat: Capturing a known adverse reaction and what works is exactly what improves future crisis safety and reduces coercion. RN role: document specifics.

opts.text: Wait until he is acutely psychotic or manic to complete the PAD so his 'real' wishes are captured
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a PAD must be authored while capable; an acute episode is when capacity is most likely impaired and the document would be invalid.

opts.text: Bypass him and have his sister complete and sign the document because he carries a schizoaffective diagnosis
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — he is capable; the diagnosis alone does not equal incapacity, and bypassing him ignores his autonomy.

opts.text: Promise him the PAD will guarantee he is never hospitalized against his will and will always override the crisis team and any court order
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a PAD cannot force or forbid hospitalization and cannot override a safety emergency or a valid commitment order; over-promising misinforms him.

opts.text: Complete the document but file it only in the clinic chart, with no copies to the agent, hospital, or crisis team
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a PAD the crisis team cannot find during an episode is useless; portability across settings is essential.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm present capacity and elicit his treatment history, values, and preferences using open questions in plain language
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Anchors the PAD in his own confirmed-capable preferences. RN role: capacity + preferences.

opts.text: Teach the components, activation/suspension, legal limits, and state-specific revocation rules honestly
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Informed understanding must precede informed choices and signatures. RN role: education.

opts.text: Facilitate selection of a mental-health agent and educate the agent about his values; involve family with his consent
opts.rat: #3 — Third. A named, informed agent is central to substituted judgment in a future crisis. RN role: agent selection + education.

opts.text: Execute the document per state law and ensure cross-setting portability and access
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Turns the conversation into an operational, accessible crisis plan. RN role: execute + make portable.

opts.text: Document objectively and contemporaneously and frame the PAD as an ongoing process to revisit
opts.rat: #5 — Closing/ongoing. Sustains alignment with his evolving values over time. RN role: document + revisit.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The nurse confirmed capacity, elicited his treatment history and preferences in plain language, taught the components/activation/limits/revocation accurately, and supported agent selection and education
opts.rat: Patient-centered, capacity-confirmed PAD creation is the standard. Supports autonomy and a valid document.

opts.text: The PAD prominently records AVOID IM haloperidol (prior acute dystonia), his preference for his LAI / oral olanzapine, preferred hospital, ECT preference, and de-escalation strategies
opts.rat: Capturing a known adverse reaction and what works directly improves future crisis safety and reduces coercion.

opts.text: The document was executed per state law, copies were placed in the chart/EHR and state registry, given to the patient and agent, and shared with the psychiatrist, preferred hospital ED, and crisis team — with a wallet card
opts.rat: Portability is the key operational success — a PAD the crisis team can actually find.

opts.text: At his next relapse the inpatient team retrieved the PAD, used his LAI/oral olanzapine, AVOIDED haloperidol, and engaged his sister/agent — shortening and de-escalating the crisis
opts.rat: Honoring a valid, applicable PAD during a crisis is the entire point — it delivers the care he chose when well.

opts.text: Alternative — the team waited until he was acutely manic and psychotic to have him complete and sign the PAD
opts.rat: ⚠️ A PAD must be authored while capable; signing during an episode, when capacity is impaired, produces an invalid document.

opts.text: Alternative — staff treated his schizoaffective diagnosis as automatic incapacity and had his sister complete the document for him
opts.rat: ⚠️ The diagnosis alone does not equal incapacity; capacity is decision-specific and presumed, and bypassing a capable patient ignores his autonomy.

opts.text: Alternative — he was told the PAD guarantees he can never be hospitalized against his wishes and always overrides the crisis team and any court order
opts.rat: ⚠️ A PAD cannot force or forbid hospitalization and cannot override a safety emergency or valid commitment; over-promising misinforms him.

opts.text: Alternative — the PAD was completed but filed only in the clinic chart; at a later ED crisis the team did not know his wishes and gave him IM haloperidol
opts.rat: ⚠️ Portability failure is the most common PAD operational error — and here it led directly to the very medication he sought to avoid.

opts.text: Alternative — the nurse pressured him toward specific choices and had him sign sections he did not understand
opts.rat: ⚠️ Coercion and uninformed signing invalidate the process; educate and support, do not pressure.

opts.text: He left empowered, with an accessible PAD, an educated agent, and the understanding that he can update it anytime while capable
opts.rat: The ideal outcome of proactive outpatient PAD creation — autonomy preserved, crisis preferences accessible, agent prepared.
